data.title: 3 Reasons Tesla Could Be Worth $600 Per Share By The End Of 2027 (Upgrade)
data.description:
Over the last few years, we’ve put out a number of articles on Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA), where we’ve come out as bulls or bears on the stock depending on market conditions.

In November of 2023, we initiated coverage with a Sell rating when the company looked expensive, followed by an upgrade in April of 2024 when the selloff looked overdone.

In January of this year, we thought TSLA looked prohibitively expensive - even taking into account the company's Robotaxis potential - and soon after, shares plunged lower as Musk’s political entanglements and broader pressure in the automotive industry put a damper on profits.

More recently, we've upgraded shares to a Hold, arguing that the company appears fairly valued by the market, based on its current businesses and the potential value of a fully operational Robotaxi segment:

While some view the company as a preposterously expensive car manufacturer, we believe that upcoming automated ride hail service could be incredibly valuable for society, and incredibly profitable for shareholders.

Since our most recent rating, there have been a number of positive developments at Tesla that we think are worth covering. When combined, we think they're enough to upgrade our view on the company from neutral to bullish.

Today, we'll outline the company's recent developments, give our outlook for the stock, and make the case that it is once again time to pile into this advanced manufacturing juggernaut.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Reason 1: Profit Inflection

The first positive development that we haven’t covered in previous articles is the company's Q2 earnings, which we thought were particularly strong.

While revenues were down roughly 12% YoY versus Q2 in 2024, and operating margins were down a little more than 200 basis points, we were encouraged by the fact that the company's gross margins and operating margins improved sequentially from recent quarters:

If you recall, Tesla has seen significant margin pressure in recent quarters as sales volumes have dropped against a relatively fixed cost base. This fixed cost base provides the company significant operational leverage when volumes and revenues are strong, but with musk's political entanglements throughout the first half of this year, alongside a price slashing campaign led by Chinese manufacturers, it led to a significant drop in quarterly margins for the company.

Now, with Q2 margins moving in the right direction on a QoQ basis, it’s possible to make the case profits have inflected back up and should continue to do so going forward. As prices continue to normalize following a discount heavy 2023 and 2024, it seems as though Tesla could be in a good position to grow earnings-per-share from here.

Another positive was the firm's service segment, which grew 17% YoY. While the segment sports lower gross profitability versus the automotive segment on a nominal basis, the growth is welcome, and this boosted quarterly net incomes.

We expect this will continue, as the company's supercharger network continues to expand, preowned inventory continues to turn over, and the in-house insurance business finds its footing.

Taking together, we’d argue that Tesla is in a materially stronger financial position now then it was earlier in the year, which is a positive

Reason 2: Musk’s Pay Package

On September 12, Tesla announced a $1 trillion pay package for CEO Elon Musk.

While large, the package is contingent on performance goals, and we believe this is a massive net positive for shareholders.

Many (including the Pope) have been quick to write this off as being gratuitous or uneconomical, but we have a different view.

Over the last few years, Elon Musk’s interest in Tesla has seemed to wane, as the company's CEO has found his attention split across multiple new ventures, including X (formally Twitter), SpaceX (SPACE), Neuralink, and a foray into politics. Since 2022, shareholders have bemoaned the lack of focus from Elon on the company, and this has notably been a period during which TSLA's share price has been stagnant.

While we're not a fan of the way that Elon Musk went about earning this pay package - i.e., complaining that he didn’t have enough of a stake in the company - there’s no question that these payouts will re-incentivize musk to get on the same page as the company's shareholders.

Here are the details.

By meeting various market cap and production targets, Musk will earn 423 million additional shares of Tesla, representing 12% of the current outstanding shares of the company.

The share vesting is split into 12 separate tranches, the first of which unlocks after Tesla reaches $2 trillion in market cap.

From there, Musk will earn an additional tranche for every $500 billion in market cap gained, up to a cap of $8.5 trillion.

The package also has various production targets, including 20 million vehicles, 20 million active full self driving subscriptions, 1 million Optimus robots, and 1 million Robotaxis in commercial use.

Finally, this pay package aligns Musk for the next 7 1/2 to 10 years, over which any earned trenches will fully vest. This demonstrably links Musk - arguably the company's greatest asset - with shareholder interests.

Of note, during negotiations with the board, Musk gave his assurances that he would wind down his political involvement and discuss succession planning.

While Elon's promises have a mixed track record - just to ask anyone who expected full self driving in 2019 - we feel as though Musk's politically active (and volatile) period is likely coming to an end.

Finally, for those who feel as though the package is overly generous to Musk, we would make the argument that if Tesla earns a market cap $8.5 trillion, it will have produced an absolutely unheard of amount of economic value for shareholders at society writ large.

As a result, we feel the compensation package materially boosts our investment thesis.

Season 3: Robotaxi Progress

The third and final reason that we feel confident once again getting bullish on Tesla has to do with how much progress the company has made since initially launching Robotaxis in Austin earlier this year.

We have followed the coverage of this service very closely, and it appears as though many think Tesla’s taxi service is safe, efficient, and well run.

Obviously, having a Tesla safety monitor in the car is a bit of a downer with respect to the eventual promise of Robotaxis and privacy, but it makes sense with respect to the broader roll-out goals.

In order to win regulatory approval to launch the Robotaxi service in new jurisdictions, the company needs to prove the service's safety and reliability. For now, having safety monitors in the car makes it easier to diagnose and fix problems, leading to a faster learning - and thus, adoption - curve.

Speaking of regulatory approval, the company recently won clearance from the Arizona Department of transportation to begin testing Robotaxi vehicles in the state, which we view as a massive win.

In our previous article on Tesla, where we compared its Robotaxi service with Google’s (GOOG) Waymo, we argued that Waymo appeared to be in a better position, given that it was in more markets and had way more miles driven.

As Tesla begins winning more approvals, we expect this Waymo lead to evaporate. Recently, on the Q2 earnings call, Musk mentioned that the company was in the process of getting regulatory permission to launch in the Bay Area, Nevada and Florida - in addition to Arizona - alongside a number of "other places":

And we're getting the regulatory permission to launch in the Bay Area, Nevada, Arizona, Florida, and a number of other places. So as we get the approvals and we prove out safety, then we will be launching autonomous ride-hailing in most of the country. And I think we'll probably have autonomous ride-hailing in probably half the population of the U.S. by the end of the year. That's at least our goal, subject to regulatory approvals. I think we'll technically be able to do it. So assuming we get regulatory approvals, it's probably addressing half of the population in the U.S. by the end of the year.

If these launches go well, then we expect that Tesla could launch in a number of metros nationwide by the end of the year, and potentially nationally by the end of 2026.

This growth trajectory would come in well above our previous bull case estimates, indicating that the fair value of Robotaxis (and Tesla on the whole) could be higher than previously anticipated.

Taken together, over the next five years, analysts are anticipating Tesla to grow EPS to $13.94 per share. This represents massive continued YoY EPS growth off of the back of Robotaxi execution and a budding Optimus business:

While neither of these businesses exist right now, Musk is no stranger to an execution challenge, and recent progress on this front leaves us optimistic.

In the event that Tesla can execute this growth path, then it appears possible that shares of Tesla could reach $600 or more by the end of 2027:

This model bakes in multiple contraction from a P/E of 225x (where we are now) to a P/E of 190 over the next 26 months, based on the fact that continued execution on the growth opportunity would likely keep the multiple inflated. It's likely we'd see some compression, but not to a drastic degree. For as long as we can remember, TSLA has been valued at these levels.

Simply put, if TSLA can achieve what analysts - and we - think it can over the next five years, then it appears as though there is considerable upside for investors.

Hence our rating upgrade.

How We're Wrong

At this point, Tesla is ultimately a 'show me' story. That is, the company needs to execute on the growth opportunity, which comes with significant technological challenges and product hurdles. If the company stumbles or loses momentum, especially with respect to regulatory progress on the Robotaxis, then shares are likely worth materially less than they are today.

This brings us to the other risk - valuation.

Again, as we’ve laid out, one can make a logical case for investing in Tesla, based on the current multiple and Robotaxi growth prospects, even with some multiple compression.

However, if the execution stumbles, it’s likely that the market will re-rate shares lower. This is a key risk simply due to how expensive the company is right now, especially vs. peers.

Make no mistake. There are significant risks to investing in Tesla at today’s price.

Summary

Overall, we think Tesla deserves to be upgraded to a Buy based on recent profitability progress in the core automotive segment, a newly "aligned" pay package with CEO Musk, and continued momentum on the automated ride hail front.

While there are risks associated with a purchase of Tesla's stock at today’s price, we think there is also material upside in the coming years if the company can execute.

Cheers¬

data.title: Prudent Financial Management And Operational Leverage Strengthens Astronics' Bull Case
data.description:
Astronics Corporation (ATRO) is on a stellar run this year, having already seen its share price rise over 170% YTD. Volatility has accompanied these gains, but every dip has been bought by investors, helping the stock continue its uptrend. The company designs and manufactures products used in the aerospace and defense industry. While a quarter of the company’s revenue comes from the Defense & Government segment, it sets itself apart from the rest through its dominance in providing cabin infrastructure to the commercial aerospace industry. If you have travelled by air, there is a good chance you have interacted with Astronics products through the in-flight entertainment (IFE) systems, in-seat power systems (ISPS), and cabin lighting and avionics. This segment powers more than two-thirds of the company’s revenue and also drives my bullish thesis on the stock.

I am optimistic on the company’s prospects not just because of its financial resurgence but also because of the way it has positioned itself to benefit from emerging air travel trends. It is set to be a major beneficiary of a potential overhaul of in-flight entertainment systems and the replacement of older aircraft with new-generation ones over the course of the next two decades. The firm’s focus on adding to its defense capabilities also bodes well at a time when defense budgets are going up across the globe.

BYOD Trend to Spur Investments

Air travel demand took 4 years to return to pre-COVID levels and continues to thrive post-recovery. As more and more people travel by air, airlines are fighting to win passengers by improving cabin infrastructure. This spending is driven partly by passenger preferences, as people prefer bringing their own devices to the aircraft rather than relying on in-flight entertainment systems.

I believe there is a good chance that most airlines will remove backseat screens in the not-too-distant future. American Airlines started this trend over 8 years ago and received mixed responses from both customers and peers. It didn’t stop them, and by early 2026, they are likely to remove the backseat screens from their Legacy Airbus A319s as well. In 2020, Etihad Airlines removed the backseat screens from 23 of its aircraft, while two years later, Malaysia Airlines did something similar with its Boeing 737-800s. In all these cases, the common denominator was the fact that a vast majority of the people brought their own entertainment devices to the plane. These airlines decided to remove the redundant screens, thus removing unnecessary weight from the aircraft while also saving on the maintenance expenses of keeping these screens functional.

Satellite-based Wi-Fi services like Starlink can now provide high-speed internet, enabling passengers to stream on their own devices rather than rely on the IFE. This aligns with the Bring Your Own Devices (BYOD) trend. I believe passengers will prefer streaming on their own devices if given the opportunity. 65% of the passengers already prefer using their own devices or in-flight entertainment purposes, according to a study by Tech Sci Research, as quoted recently by Simple Flying on why airlines do not want to spend on backseat screens anymore.

I must add here that this trend is more likely to be adopted on short-haul flights using narrow body aircraft, where passengers are more likely to sacrifice on in-flight entertainment options to save money, and airlines are more concerned about reducing costs. Astronics has an equal revenue distribution between narrow-body and wide-body aircraft, so it benefits from this potential change. As this trend materializes, the modern personal devices will demand quality infrastructure, including consistent power and connectivity. This is where Astronics comes in. It provides the right infrastructure to power this BYOD trend. The company controls more than 90% market share in In-Seat Power Systems! Even when backseat screens are phased out, ATRO stays relevant as personal entertainment systems still need power to run. In fact, they need an even more reliable power infrastructure, making this shift a positive catalyst for the company.

In addition to a reliable power source, BYOD will also require a reliable Wi-Fi system. Astronics was the first company to bring Wi-Fi 6E-enabled cabin wireless Access Point (CWAP) to aircraft through its product CABINAXe. CABINAXe not only solves the problem of sharing bandwidth between closely spaced passengers but also operates at a spectrum that does not interfere with the aircraft’s own communication systems. When airlines opt for internet service providers like Starlink, they need a reliable way to distribute that high-speed internet among passengers in a confined space. CABINAXe helps achieve exactly that.

The company generated 51% of its sales from the In-Flight Entertainment & Connectivity (IFEC) segment. As passenger behavior shifts towards a more connected experience, this segment will continue to be the company’s primary growth engine.

Benefitting From Growth Across the Fleet

Another factor that helps fuel spending on cabin infrastructure is aircraft innovation. According to Airbus, 34% of the current global fleet consists of new-generation aircraft. These offer 25% better fuel efficiency and lower carbon emissions. I have already pointed out above that airlines care about making short-haul flights cheaper, so a 25% fuel efficiency is a big deal. The money thus saved can be diverted toward improving passenger experience without sacrificing margins – something Airlines often do to attract more passengers.

The fact that 34% of the global fleet is new-generation doesn’t directly boost Astronics' revenues; it just points to a new trend, which is significant. The existing global fleet of 24,730 is likely to grow to 49,210 by the end of 2044. This includes 43,420 new deliveries!

In other words, most of the existing older generation of aircraft will be retired over the next two decades, replaced by new-generation aircraft requiring modern infrastructure: infrastructure that Astronics provides. This, together with an anticipated 3.6% compound annual growth rate in global air traffic by 2050, offers the company a perfect opportunity to increase its commercial aerospace revenues.

ATRO derives 68% of its sales from commercial aerospace, which is equally distributed between narrow-body and wide-body aircraft. Wide-body aircraft tend to offer higher content per plane, resulting in better margins. Narrow-body aircraft, which are growing faster than the wide-body versions, tend to offer higher volumes. ATRO benefits from both through its 50/50 revenue distribution between both aircraft types. More importantly, ATRO’s ISPS powers 80% of all the wide-body aircraft globally, thus ensuring it enjoys a large piece of the high-margin pie in In-Seat Power System deployment.

The company’s Q2 financials also show a healthy growth in bookings from $557.3 million in 2021 to $841.8 million by Q2 2025 (TTM). The fact that the backlog has increased at a slightly higher rate shows OEMs like Boeing and Airbus continue to produce at a higher production rate, increasing the demand for cabin infrastructure.

Astronics’ impressive backlog, a strong footprint within the in-flight entertainment niche, and evolving consumer preferences in the commercial aerospace segment all make its business fundamentals very promising. These factors endorse the management’s recent upward revision of their 2025 revenue guidance to a range of $840 million to $860 million. Moreover, the management has forecasted revenues to reach $956.5 million by 2028, which I find very realistic given many of the recent developments. What also makes me optimistic about ATRO’s projections is the potential opportunities for their defense operations, which I will discuss in detail.

Focus on Defense Capabilities

We are witnessing a widespread increase in global defense spending, which has soared to unprecedented levels. Global military expenditure in 2024 stood at roughly $2.5 trillion, marking a steep rise across Europe and the United States. The “Golden Dome” defense system suggested by President Donald Trump could spur immense spending in the sector. With an estimated cost of $175 billion up to project initiation in 2029, followed by an anticipated expenditure of $831 billion over the next 2 decades, the aerospace and defense industry players will garner attention in the years to come. This will surely extend to OEMs like Astronics, who provide critical communication systems, safety components, and test equipment to large manufacturers.

On that front, the U.S. Army’s Future Long-Range Assault Aircraft (FLRAA) program also carries a lot of significance. This program aims to enhance the military’s existing combat capabilities by replacing roughly 2,000 black hawk helicopters in the coming years. Costs associated with this program are estimated at around $70 billion.

In fact, during the Jefferies Mining and Industrials Conference on September 3, 2025, the company reiterated its stance on mission-critical defense systems. Astronics would leverage its modern electronic circuit breaker capabilities that will replace the more traditional thermal mechanical breaker systems. Most notably, Astronics is tied to a deal with aerospace manufacturer Bell Textron, which has further strengthened the long-standing partnership between the two entities. Under the terms of this deal, Astronics will supply advanced power systems and solutions for Bell’s aircraft fleet, including Bell 525, Bell 360 Invictus, and V-280 Valor, through Astronics CorePower® solutions. Chairman, President & CEO Peter Gundermann highlighted the company’s $90 million development spend that began in 2024. According to initial forecasts, the company will end up extending circa $90 million by 2026, which would also mark the first flight of the FLRAA program.

The above graph shows that Defense & Government contracts contributed 22% to ATRO’s revenue stream during the 12 months ending in June 2025. The company’s overall revenue mix is more tilted towards its commercial aerospace operations, which have been contributing close to 70% to revenues, on average. Trends in defense spending highlighted above present an opportunity for ATRO management to further diversify their revenue mix and curtail their over-reliance on the commercial aerospace segment. By increasing the proportionate revenues from the defense segment, ATRO could also mitigate risks associated with commercial aerospace operations that are sensitive to consumers’ evolving preferences.

Envoy Aerospace Acquisition

Astronics Corporation announced its acquisition of Envoy Aerospace in July 2025, penning down a deal for $8 million. Envoy Aerospace is a highly reputable Organization Designation Authorization (ODA) services provider. Envoy Aerospace helps clients attain U.S. Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) certifications, and is composed of a team of professionals who will now join the Astronics team.

This collaboration would add Envoy’s existing customers to Astronics' portfolio. The collective customer base will benefit from advanced ODA services to obtain Supplemental Type Certificates (STCs) and Parts Manufacturer Approvals (PMAs), as per FAA requirements. It is important to highlight that there is a shortage of such ODA services in the industry, and hence, this acquisition provides Astronics with a competitive edge.

Financials

In the first half of the year, ATRO reported $410.6 million in revenue, a 7% YoY jump, keeping the company on track for its 2025 revenue projections of $820 million to $860 million. Sales growth was driven by a strong backlog of $599.2 million entering into 2025, and was predominantly concentrated across functions within the aerospace segment.

Cost of products sold was $296.9 million, resulting in approximately 28% gross margins for the first six months. However, it is important to note that such high gross margins resulted from a change in the presentation of R&D expenses in the first quarter of 2025. These costs were previously part of the costs of products sold, which resulted in lower gross profits. The company now presents them as a separate line item under the gross profits.

Operating profits for the first half of 2025 stood at $17.9 million, a 94% YoY increase compared with $9.2 million during the first half of 2024. Such expansion of operating profits came on the back of higher operating leverage achieved by the company, backed by strong volumetric growth which drove the topline upwards. It also led to a net income of around $11 million for the first half, completing the company’s turnaround after multiple years of losses. Stability in supply chain functions and process automations to cut labor costs were the key drivers behind the operating leverage of 53% for the aerospace segment. It is important to note that the company did not incur any major capital expenditure to achieve the purpose. A larger proportion of fixed costs magnified the profitability ratios for the aerospace segment, which contributes more than 90% to overall sales.

Balance sheet liquidity continues to be a strong point for Astronics, as current assets stand at 65% of total assets. Through effective balance sheet management, the company was also able to bring down its net interest expense to $6.2 million compared to $11.6 million during the first half of 2024. ATRO has also managed its working capital changes, excluding cash & marketable securities, very efficiently throughout 2024 and the first half of 2025. This, along with higher operating leverage and volumetric growth, facilitated a turnaround in the operating cash flow position as well, which had remained in the negative territory in the prior 3 years.

Astronics' unlevered cash flow position has also been solidifying in recent quarters, providing solid grounds for investors to stay optimistic. The company currently has $43.4 million of unlevered FCF on a TTM basis, which marks a significant improvement in the coverage ratio. Going forward, such financial discipline is a necessity given that ATRO carries over $159 million in long-term debt and close to $31.5 million in long-term operating lease liabilities on its balance sheet.

Another recent favorable development relates to ATRO’s $165 million convertible notes issued at the backend of 2024. There was a widespread belief among market participants regarding the potential dilution risk associated with these 5.5% 2030 notes. In share count terms, it amounted to an additional 7.2 million shares. During the second week of September, ATRO management carried out a successful refinancing of these convertible notes. As ATRO registered a more than 170% jump in share price during 2025, management viewed these 5.5% 2030 notes to be deep in the money. Hence, they decided to repurchase 80% of the notes, i.e. $132 million worth of principal, at a price of $285 million. Hence, the dilution risk relating to the 2030 notes is now limited to just 1.4 million shares.

This purchase was financed through 3 sources, i.e. $11 million cash, $85 million borrowings from an interest-bearing Asset-Based Revolver (ABR) facility, and the rest from a new $225 million 0% 2031 convertible notes issue. These notes are non-interest-bearing and carry an initial conversion price of $54.87, which represents a 25% conversion premium.

Valuation

ATRO has been a star performer this year with more than 170% YTD gain, comfortably beating both the S&P 500’s (SPY) 16% gain and the SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF’s (XAR) 39% gain. Among individual stocks, I have identified three peers namely Collins Aerospace, a subsidiary of RTX Corporation (RTX), Docummun Incorporated (DCO), and TransDigm Group Incorporated (TDG).

Since the In-Seat Power System is where Astronics dominates, this is where I will begin the comparison. Collins Aerospace is a company that has a strong overlap with Astronics in this segment. However, Astronics serves over 290 airlines and specializes in providing this infrastructure, focusing on flexibility and passenger preferences. While Collins Aerospace also does the same, it is less dominant. Also, Collins Aerospace is focused on leveraging the scale of RTX and has bigger fish to fry, especially in installing mission-critical systems. Astronics therefore does not have to worry about protecting its market share from its closest competitor. Collins Aerospace dominates the in-flight entertainment market, again leveraging the scale of RTX to bundle its products with other systems that it installs in passenger planes. Since I have already pointed out that in-flight entertainment systems could move from backseat screens to individual devices, resulting in a greater demand for ISPS, a segment that Astronics already dominates, there is again no danger of losing market share.

As for the other two peers, TDG and DCO, there are many similarities in the way their business is structured. TDG has a similar revenue mix, generating 60% of its revenues from commercial aerospace. For DCO, the same number stands at 40%. Astronics is more skewed toward the commercial aerospace segment as a percentage of revenue at 68%, but these businesses do not directly compete in each other’s market share. For instance, DCO only provides certain components necessary in the lighting or power systems within the cabin; it doesn’t have a product like Astronics does. TDG is a holding company and owns smaller companies that provide components that go into in-seat power systems. However, it again doesn’t compete with the type of infrastructure that Astronics provides. Despite this, these two companies are the two closest in terms of where they stand in the aerospace industry, so they offer a good comparison when determining Astronics' valuation.

Astronics has comfortably outperformed both DCO and TDG since the beginning of the year, as shown below.

This outperformance has helped the stock close its valuation gap with these peers, which means it is no longer as undervalued as it was earlier in the year. On a forward P/E basis, Astronics, with a multiple of 26.0,9 has already surpassed DCO at 25.18 but still has some way to reach TDG’s P/E ratio of 35.15. The Price/Sales of 1.83 has similarly surpassed DCO’s 1.75 though just like in the case of P/E ratio, it still has a long way to go to catch up to TDG’s 8.80 multiple.

The tricky question in this scenario is not whether the momentum for Astronics stock will continue, but whether the stock is a buy at the current price. It is trading well above its 50-day moving average, and judging by the strong recovery after the most recent convertible notes offering, as well as earnings, I am inclined to think waiting for a pullback is not a smart move.

If one buys the stock at the current market price, the question that remains then is what is the upside from current levels?

I have used different forward-looking multiples to estimate a target price range for Astronics. Since a price hike in shares during 2025, Astronics is currently trading at a 1.83x forward price-to-sales multiple. After management’s upward revision in the 2025 annual sales forecast between $840 million to $860 million, the target price works out to be above $44 a share. Similarly, the price target based on 2025 EPS estimates of $1.64 turns out to be $42.79, assuming a 26.09x forward P/E ratio. However, as we can see the stock is currently trading very close to these estimates, especially following the refinancing through 2031 0% convertible notes.

Hence, I added more rigour to my analysis by applying the same P/E multiple of 26.09x to 2026 EPS estimate of $2.01. This results in a price target of $52.44 by the end of 2026, which is very close to the conversion price of recently issued convertible notes.

Risks

The risk profile of Astronics appears limited, but I have identified three factors that investors must know before taking exposure in the stock. The first one is litigation. In February 2025, Astronics was asked to pay up by a UK court as part of the Astronics vs. Lufthansa Technik Case. The amount is just below $12 million and well covered by the company’s cash flows, even when legal fees and other expenses are added to it. The lawsuit in France is still pending, but is unlikely to cause any major headache. A favorable result in one jurisdiction is not a strong enough reason to influence a case in another jurisdiction; such is the nature of lawsuits in the aerospace industry. The litigation risk, therefore, is minimal.

The second risk relates to the recently issued 2031 0% convertible notes for the refinancing of a previous issue in 2024. Although the conversion price of $54.87 offers more favorable terms to existing shareholders, the shareholding dilution risk still exists. Astronics has made an announcement to buy $25 million worth of capped calls to mitigate such dilution risks in the future. The strike price associated with those capped calls is $83.41, which offers a significant amount of protection from dilution risk. But at the same time, it poses refinancing risk to Astronics in case the share price does not rise enough. Hence, investors should closely monitor movements in the share price in the upcoming quarter.

Finally, I would like to highlight risks associated with Astronics' operating leverage. As mentioned earlier, operating leverage has played a pivotal role in magnifying profitability ratios for Astronics in the recent quarters. But it also comes with a potential downside, as the ratios might take a bigger hit in case sales growth is lackluster. This is the reason why Astronics is in need of consistent operational and financial discipline to build upon the current turnaround. Again, it will be interesting to see the financial disclosures in the upcoming quarters that will provide more context on the effectiveness of management’s operational strategy.

Recommendation

With a solid demand backlog and an impressive financial turnaround, I feel very optimistic about Astronics. Share price has delivered over 170% returns on a YTD basis. The company is on course to turn profitable in 2025, which will strengthen investor confidence and provide support to the share price. Despite the highlighted risks, prospects for Astronics look good as the company is undergoing a financial and operational turnaround. Fleet upgrade plans within commercial and military aerospace markets offer encouragement, where Astronics can build upon its existing long-standing business relationships. Based on these factors, I will rate Astronics a Buy.

data.title: Cleveland-Cliffs: Political Risk At Its Finest
data.description:
Over the years, I have been following various businesses that depend heavily on political decisions. This is especially true for companies operating in the Energy and Basic Materials sectors, where decisions made by different governments often have strong implications for shareholder returns.

As dependent as companies operating in these industries are on political factors, however, their management teams do their best to avoid fixating on the outcome of certain decisions as the sole factor of success or demise of their companies.

In the case of Cleveland-Cliffs Inc. (NYSE:CLF) we see this rhetoric being dialed up, and it appears that shareholders should be anticipating a positive outcome of a policy or government decision each and every quarter. That was one of the reasons why I turned Neutral on the stock a long time ago and just recently said that it's not worth it to try to call the bottom in the CLF share price.

Since I last covered CLF and laid out my reasoning why trying to call the bottom was not worth the effort, the stock is now up by more than 66%. To some short-term speculators this might seem like an attractive opportunity, but recent performance simply confirms my thesis that CLF has become a stock that is way too speculative for long-term investors to consider.

The first reason for that is quite simple - volatility. Even though CLF is up by more than 66% in a matter of months, the stock still underperformed its major competitors in the U.S. over the past 5-year period. Not only that, but the performance gap is very wide and even without calculating the variance of daily returns, it is obvious that CLF stock has been more volatile than those of Nucor Corporation (NUE) and Steel Dynamics, Inc. (STLD).

Consequently, CLF remains priced at a significant discount to both NUE and STLD, which by itself means that investors betting on a turnaround of CLF could expect significant upside.

The last reported quarter in July gave those optimists hope that CLF might be finally turning the corner, as results came slightly above analysts' estimates.

Although this is certainly a possibility, to me the last reported quarter simply confirmed my thesis that too much emphasis is being put on political decisions and that CLF would most likely continue to underperform its competitors over the long term.

Tariffs and Trade Restrictions

During the Q2 2025 earnings release, it was the better-than-expected results that caught investors' attention and as a result we saw a strong share price response. Of course, this reaction was also due to the recently implemented tariffs, which are now beginning to bear fruit and should show on Cleveland-Cliffs' bottom line over the coming quarter.

The state of the automotive sector in the U.S. is one of the key factors that would play a role in CLF's future performance. That is why the investor presentation during the quarter was heavily focused on tariffs and their impact on demand.

For example, more domestically produced light vehicles are anticipated to support demand for automotive steel and allow CLF to exert its pricing power in the sector.

Tariffs implemented on Brazilian pig iron imports are also expected to give CLF certain competitive advantages over some of its peers as the company now relies on its HBI plant in Toledo.

Other relevant news in trade enforcement is the very important 50% tariff that will go into place on Brazilian pig iron, starting August 1. Cleveland-Cliffs does not rely on imported pig iron at all. We have our own hot-briquetted iron facility in Toledo, Ohio, but several of our EAF competitors do rely on important pig iron.

Source: CLF Q2 2025 Earnings Transcript.

While these are all good points that investors should consider, it was quite surprising how tariff-dominated the narrative around CLF has become. In addition to everything said above, CLF management has also stressed the importance of Canada implementing trade restrictions—perhaps due to the company's recent acquisition of Stelco, which is geographically exposed to the market.

Our message is very clear and easy to understand. If Prime Minister Carney and his cabinet really want to have a steel industry in Canada, they should put in place significant trade protections. Then they will have a strong domestic steel industry in Canada, able to support a vibrant domestic Canadian market.

Source: CLF Q2 2025 Earnings Transcript.

Some comments made about the Chair of the Federal Reserve were a bit surprising and it seems that CLF's success in the automated sector also depends on the appointment of a new Chairman of the Fed.

Once Chairman Jerome Powell is gone, and that's not a matter of when, not if, and as soon as interest rates come down by 50 or 75 basis points, the automotive sector will take off again. Demand is there. But this Fed Chairman will not act. So we need a new Fed Chairman appointed as soon as possible.

Source: CLF Q2 2025 Earnings Transcript.

All further supports my thesis that future shareholder returns are increasingly linked to the outcome of very different political decisions—from tariffs implemented on imports in the U.S. to trade restrictions in Canada and even the appointment of a new Chairman of the Fed.

A Turning Point?

Higher realized prices were among the key quantifiable positives during the quarter as many investors have been eagerly awaiting a shift in the downtrend in recent years.

Q2 results were largely driven by better realized pricing, cost reductions and record shipments.

Source: CLF Q2 2025 Earnings Transcript.

As expected, the quarterly realized pricing has been linked to the realized gross margins of Cliffs (see the graph below). Unfortunately, the pricing during Q2 2025 was not enough to swing CLF into a positive gross margin territory and based on the historical relationship below we would anticipate a break-even on a gross margin basis at prices of around $1,100 per ton.

CLF's share price, however, is already accounting for a potential improvement in the second half of 2025 as the price-to-sales ratio has moved to levels that we last saw when CLF reported positive gross margins.

Therefore, in order to justify the CLF's current share price, investors should anticipate average quarterly realized prices to continue improving over the coming quarters and at a much higher rate than they did in the first half of 2025.

Even if that does occur, CLF competitors would also enjoy significant profitability tailwinds, while their positive net income figures would act as downside protection in the event of a more adverse event for the U.S. and global economy. That is why I remain optimistic about the performance of some of CLF's major competitors and just recently Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.A), (BRK.B) has made a similar bet.

Conclusion

As investors rejoice at the fact that CLF recently delivered better-than-expected results, I remain cautious about the stock's attractiveness due to the significant political risk involved. While CLF cloud certainty outperforms its peers in the near term, doing so consistently over the long run appears unlikely. Moreover, the share price is already accounting for some improvements in the second half of 2025, which, in my view, significantly reduces any upside through the rest of the year.

data.title: Takeda: Excellent Pipeline Kaizen - 6 Drugs, $20 Billion Annually
data.description:
Introduction

Takeda (NYSE:TAK) is currently a unique biopharma case that may lead to opportunities for investors. While the company is facing significant valuation issues, particularly after the costly Shire acquisition, the company has performed deft kaizen to improve their pipeline. In fact, Takeda has quietly developed six phase 3 therapies that have the opportunity to disrupt competition or provide a first-mover advantage in key, profitable niches. Takeda expects to earn up to $20 billion in revenues upon approval and commercialization. These peak sales estimates, included in the following analysis are taken from Takeda’s estimations provided at their JPM 2025 presentation. However, these are lofty figures so I will determine whether they may end up true or not. The key is to analyze each molecule individually, particularly as Takeda has been extremely selective in choosing this pipeline. Let’s take a look.

Zasocitinib

Zasocitinib is a small molecule Tyk2 kinase inhibitor developed using computational chemistry to allow for a new best-in-class psoriasis treatment. This therapy is set to disrupt and outperform current best-in-class inhibitor Sotyktu, which currently earns Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY) about $250 million per year as of 2024 and growing at a 40% annual growth rate. As orally administered small molecules, these drugs look to displace the far more costly TNF inhibitors such as AbbVie’s Humira (ABBV) and Amgen’s Enbrel (AMGN) or IL inhibitor mAbs such as Novartis's Cosentyx (NVS). Zasocitinib’s advantage is that the molecule is far more selective, and any advantages in efficacy will be financially supportive, particularly as the company plans to expand into numerous immune indications.

The opportunity is great, but yet to reflect in the share price. One reason for this is that while the selectivity or efficacy shown is “biologic-level”, the huge competition in the immunology market will provide fierce competition that will be a tough battle for debt-burdened Takeda. Basically: do they have the marketing strength to get doctors to prescribe? However, if Takeda succeeds, the $3-$6 billion target will likely be met or exceeded, with IL and TNF inhibitors account for at least $20 billion in sales on their own based on the drugs listed above.

Peak Sales Estimate: $3-$6 billion

Rusfertide

PTG-300, or rusfertide, is a peptide hormone replacement injection used to treat the rare cancer polycythemia vera. As an orphan drug, and first-in-class type of therapy, rusfertide offers both financial and scientific benefits for Takeda. Unmet need and orphan status operational standards apply, i.e. smaller patient populations and reduced trial costs, but higher therapy cost, allowing for investments to be recouped alongside partner Protagonist Therapeutics (PTGX) thanks to their worldwide collaboration and license agreement. Considering the upfront partnership cost was just $300 million for a 50/50 profit share (royalties to PTGX ex-US), blockbuster status will be a boon to Takeda.

An interesting development is the sharply rising share price of PTGX contrasted against the flat price of TAK over the past year or so. Considering the lasting impact of this therapy, the partnership with Protagonist (if Takeda can execute), and the potential success of the peptide therapy modality, I find the value here to be significant. Perhaps an acquisition of PTGX will allow for sales estimates to increase as there will no longer be a 50/50 split.

Peak Sales Estimates: $1-$2 billion, per Takeda management.

Oveporexton

Another first-in-class therapy, oveporexton (TAK-861) is an oral OX2R agonist to treat narcolepsy type 1. This is the first treatment for the disease as current approvals focus merely on dealing with symptoms. Data has been quite strong across all primary and secondary endpoints, and the majority of patients achieved near-normal ranges for measured indicators. Analysts and Takeda expect blockbuster status to be quite achievable, particularly as there is room for expanded indications across all orexin-based disease classes like narcolepsy type 2 and idiopathic hypersomnia. These patients will likely be pleased to switch from current symptom management treatments and into a new, safer modality (no scheduling necessary!).

As an oral small molecule, this means there will be high profit margins, enough to begin tackling the company’s debt issues if approval is earned and commercialization succeeds. However, competition will be fierce, with legacy giant Jazz Pharmaceuticals (JAZZ) also developing an orexin-based therapy, and biotech Alkermes (ALKS) as well. But, these are still in early stage trials and so Takeda should benefit from first mover advantage for the meantime. Again, sales estimates are likely conservative as the sleep disorder market is large and faces issues with current treatments being unfavorable for patients.

Peak Sales Estimate: $2-$3 billion

Fazirsiran

With a key partnership with Alylam (ALNY) upcoming rival Arrowhead Pharmaceuticals (ARWR), Takeda is set to enter the oligonucleotide fray with RNAi therapy fazirsiran. This therapy uses the body’s natural mechanisms to degrade mutant proteins in those who are suffering from alpha-1 antitrpsin deficiency, or AATD. There are a few benefits to this therapy. One, the disease exhibits a buildup of misfolded proteins in the liver, leading to disease and fibrosis, and oligonucleotides like the siRNA of this therapy are best administered with an IV in lipid nanoparticles to the liver. Arrowhead, as a close follower to Alnylam, also employs GalNaC technology to coat the nucleotides for increased uptake into cells and reduced breakdown of the RNA in the bloodstream. This should limit adverse effects.

Thanks to another 50/50 profit share, Takeda is set to benefit from this next-generational therapeutic modality. Although, as with Alnylam, there may be plenty of revenue growth but the profits may be more difficult to come by due to the higher cost of manufacturing and developing these complex therapeutics. The small patient population helps as specialty products demand a premium. I find the estimates to be fairly accurate for this therapy, only as Alnylam and peers still barely reach $5 billion in RNA-based therapies after half a decade of approvals combined. The rollout may be slower than expected due to manufacturing needs, so keep that in mind.

Peak Sales Estimate: $1-$3 billion

Mezagitamab

Takeda’s last key late-stage offering potential is anti-CD-38 antibody, mezagitamab, which is a next-generation antibody treatment specifically for the rare autoimmune disease, immune thrombocytopenia (ITP). The drug is more targeted and selective for CD38, allowing for some potential to outcompete off-label or expanded indications of Roche’s (OTCQX:RHHBY) rituximab/Rituxan or Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) /Genmab’s (GMAB) daratumumab/Darzalex.

There are a few crucial points to consider for this therapy, alongside the key breakthrough and fast track designations. First, the antibody is highly selective for CD-38, allowing for reduced side effects and higher efficacy in CD-38 mediated diseases, which are primarily related to blood disorders. Second, antibody therapies are fairly mature, but profitable entities, which will allow for stable profit generation by Takeda. By starting as a second line treatment for a rare disease, the company will have limited competition and revenues, but will be able to use profits to expand their indications into larger patient populations. This should be a fairly easy target to meet with Takeda’s current experience.

Peak Sales Estimate: $1-$3 billion

Elritercept

Once again, Takeda has partnered with a biotech company to access global rights to a novel, potentially best-in-class therapeutic. This time, TAK has partnered with Keros Therapeutics (KROS) to develop a drug that promotes blood cell production to treat myelodysplastic syndromes. The current competition is Bristol Myers Squibb’s luspatercept/Reblozyl that earns over $1 billion per year, but may be outcompeted by the efficacy of elritercept that is able to increase both blood cell and platelet production (rather than just blood cells with luspatercept). While it will require a solid commercialization strategy, the opportunity is present to eat away at BMS’s blockbuster market share or grow their own niche. However, we must continue to look at the P3 data to truly weigh the competitive potential. While one of the riskier bets of the bunch, the reward may also exceed expectations for that very reason.

Peak Sales Estimate: $1-$3 billion.

Financial Analysis

So, considering the highest peak sales, we reach $20 billion in revenues. Timing is important too, as these therapies will approach these peaks around the year 2030 to 2031, which is when cash cow Entyvio begins to lose patent protection. Takeda has also faced headwinds from the loss of exclusivity for Shire’s Vyvanse which has led to flat revenues over the past five years when not considering the top-line growth due to the acquisition. This is similar to the period between 2013 and 2019 where Takeda lost exclusivity on several blockbusters, but failed to have a strong pipeline to replace those revenues. Now however, Takeda has a much stronger late stage pipeline that should come into play over the next few years.

Along with weak topline organic growth of sales, Takeda has struggled with margin expansion. While EBITDA margins have remained fairly stable around the low 30s%, net income margins have been quite weak, often negative in some quarters. The poor bottom end performance has been due to the attempted restructuring of internal and Shire operations, along with costs associated with the highly advanced pipeline. Six major candidates in late stage trials is no spare change, but financial conservatism is not the company’s strong point since the firm attempted a global rollout in the 2000s. As part of the restructuring, Takeda has sold off numerous non-core assets, so with time this should improve the revenue mix towards higher margin products, the ones I have discussed in the pipeline especially, so I do expect margin expansion to be possible over the coming years.

Takeda will certainly benefit from increased profit generation from the current assets in development. Most important will be debt reduction, with current leverage above safe levels at 3.2x net debt/EBITDA (TTM). Investors have not been treated well, as valuations have fallen and risk increased over the years. There is one positive: stable cash flow generation. This highlights the ship is righting itself and Takeda’s balance sheet should improve from here. Once debt is reduced, the dividend should continue growing steadily, and shares can be bought back, all leading to a rising valuation.

Outlook

If Takeda is successful in their endeavor to reach $20 billion in revenues from the six discussed therapeutic products by 2030-31, there is no doubt significant returns are possible for investors. None of this success is priced in at a current 1.5x P/S (LTM). However, the math is extremely risky still until these drugs are approved. This is why the valuation remains suppressed as Takeda investors want a show, not tell situation. Let’s break down the opportunity.

Scenario 1: 100% Success

In this scenario, Takeda has all therapies approved and meets the $20 billion revenue target, let's say in 2031. These therapies are both strategically and scientifically sound, allowing for operating income margins to average 25%. Why? Some are small molecules that cost little to manufacture, but will hold leading market positions. Others are antibodies that provide significant aid to patients, deserving a premium. Others are new modalities or rare diseases that also demand premiums due to the innovation and small patient populations that support high prices to allow a return on investment. This means Takeda may earn $5 billion in additional profit annually, rounded down to $4 billion to account for taxes.

However, we must also remove about $1.2 billion in dividend payments. This brings total profit down to $2.8 billion. Added to current therapy earnings of ~$1 billion, that is $3.8 billion. At a P/E of 20, this is a $76 billion market cap, or 10% per year in returns over five years from now. Fiddling around with the numbers, such as removing profit for debt reduction, or increasing the valuation some, generally offset each other so this figure seems to be a fairly manageable target. However, Scenario 2 will show how it is a bit risky to go by management’s expectations.

Scenario 2: 50% Success

The key issues at hand are that not all therapies are guaranteed to be approved. Therapies can also underperform expectations, particularly due to the competitive rivals I discussed for each therapy. Project Alpha from MIT calculates the probability of success for clinical trials, and found that there is a 67.8% chance for a drug to go from Phase 3 to Approval. We must also lower that due to post approval risks such as manufacturing issues, commercialization stalls, and broader competition. Therefore, we can conservatively discount Takeda’s outlook by 50%. One of the biggest reasons why one would favor this scenario is due to Takeda’s weak reorganization after the Shire acquisition. This is an essential fact for why there is no premium attributed to the company at present. $1.9 billion in earnings in five years is in fact a -5% per year return if the valuation falls to 20x P/E.

Scenario 3: 75% Success

The problem with this scenario is that it does not account for the scientific grounds of each therapy. Takeda has chosen well. The pipeline is incredibly efficient and checks off many factors that increase the probability of success. For example, zasocitinib was computationally developed for incredible binding ability to the already de-risked and proven TYK2 inhibition class. So there are already approved molecules, but this drug is potentially safer and more effective due to reduced off-target effects. Also, oveporexton, fazisiran, and rusfertide all leverage the fact that they are first-in-class therapies that fill unmet needs, allowing for some flexibility if the data is not perfect. In rare diseases, the Project Alpha data shows that rare diseases in Blood and Clotting and Immunological indications have over 70% chance of approval from P3. This highlights the ability of scientific strategy to fulfill operational success. Takeda has done well, so the biggest risk remains commercialization.

Conclusion

To conclude, I believe a success rate of 75% may be more accurate. This will lead to about $2.85 billion in earnings per year. If the valuation rightly increases significantly, and margins are above my conservative 25% rate for these therapies, then returns can be in the mid double digits from current levels. My Scenario 3 is therefore what I feel more confident about. My strategy will be to consider accumulating shares at current levels and take advantage of continued releases of positive data. If this happens there may be a considerable surge in valuation and share price as the market learns of the opportunity. However, I will be keen to trim on any bull runs as there is downside risk due to Scenario 2.

The sell on good news and buy back at lower valuations ploy will likely be the most conservative and profitable strategy. It’s always best to not get too attached to any biopharma stock, even if you are an income investor enjoying Takeda’s high dividend. Beware the company making the correct choice and not increasing the dividend significantly over time. The total returns will be greater overall and the future will look bright if debt can be reduced and they continue to adeptly generate a high-quality pipeline. Anyway, let me know what you think of this strategy.

Thanks for reading. Feel free to share your thoughts below.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Confluent: A Fantastic 'Growth At A Reasonable Price' Investment
data.description:
Jerome Powell just said out loud what was on many of our minds: stocks appear to be quite expensive. And yet, taking this as an overarching blanket statement doesn't capture the true reality. While the S&P 500 is indeed sitting at stretched multiples, it's really the large-cap side of the house that is driving the gains, while small- and mid-cap growth stocks are still sitting at quite buyable levels. In my view, investors who are looking for downside protection should increasingly, by rotating, allocate away from large-cap and into small-cap.

Confluent, Inc. (NASDAQ:CFLT) is a great way to do so. The data streaming company, which helps its customers store their data and prepare it for use in cloud applications, has seen its share price decline by ~30% this year on worries over growth deceleration. And while Confluent may no longer be a growth star, its attractive combination of 20%+ revenue growth, rising operating margins, and cheap valuation multiples makes it a great rebound candidate.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Confluent in May, when the stock was still trading above $20 per share. Since then, Confluent has taken a fresh leg lower and missed out on the sharp rally in the S&P 500, which to me accentuates the "growth at a reasonable price" appeal of this stock. And despite the mounting pessimism here, Confluent raised its full-year expectations. Amid all of these factors, I'm reiterating my "Buy" opinion on this stock.

To me, these are the core reasons to be bullish on Confluent:

  • Substantial $100 billion TAM. Confluent believes that the global addressable market for data streaming and governance sits at over $100 billion, which gives the company ample room for expansion despite the broad set of players in the space (ranging from peers like Cloudera to large cloud giants like AWS).
  • Blue-chip customers. The company's strong reputation is affirmed by the blue-chip nature of its customer base, which includes some of the largest names in their respective industries, such as Netflix, Inc. (NFLX), Citigroup Inc. (C), Walmart Inc. (WMT), Square (SQ), and The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS).
  • High-margin recurring revenue. Subscription revenue makes up over 90% of Confluent's revenue base, stabilizing its business and providing for substantial opportunities to unlock economies of scale.
  • Making strides on operating margin. The company just crossed into positive operating margin territory last year, which is respectable for a company hitting over >20% y/y growth in subscription revenue.
  • Significant liquidity. Confluent has nearly $2 billion of cash on its balance sheet, giving the company plenty of firepower to chase growth opportunities.

The biggest draw for Confluent, however, is the stock's undemanding valuation. At current share prices just north of $19, Confluent trades at a market cap of $6.81 billion. Netting off the $1.94 billion of cash and $1.09 billion of convertible debt on Confluent's most recent balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $5.97 billion.

Meanwhile, for the remainder of FY25, Confluent has raised the low end of its subscription revenue guidance to $1.105 billion, from $1.10 billion previously. Wall Street, meanwhile, is expecting $1.15 billion in total revenue this year (which implies roughly flat services revenue of ~$42 million from the prior year), or 19% y/y growth.

For FY26, Street expectations go to $1.34 billion in revenue, or 16% y/y growth. This positions Confluent's valuation multiples at:

  • 5.2x EV/FY25 revenue.
  • 4.4x EV/FY26 revenue.

We should consider the fact that a number of other SaaS companies that have a high-teens/~20% growth profile trade at either high-single digit or low-teens multiples. Good examples are HubSpot, Inc. (HUBS), ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW), AppFolio, Inc. (APPF), and Atlassian Corporation (TEAM):

In my view, we have a great opportunity to scoop up shares of Confluent while they're still trading at a very reasonable price relative to the company's own growth profile. Stay long here and use the recent dip to buy.

Q2 download

Let's now go through Confluent's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Confluent's revenue grew 20% y/y to $282.3 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $278.4 million (+18% y/y) by a two-point margin.

Now, it is true that Confluent's revenue has been decelerating, which is part of the reason that investors are pushing back on the stock. Subscription revenue growth of 21% y/y in Q2 decelerated 5 points from 26% growth in Q1.

The company attributed this deceleration to customers' growing desire to optimize IT budgets and rein in consumption and spending. Per CEO Jay Kreps' remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

I'd like to start by sharing some observations on our cloud business. In Q2, our larger customers continued their optimization efforts and adopted new use cases in a more measured pace. While we are confident that this elevated level of optimization will eventually subside, our outlook for the second half assumes consumption growth notably below what we've seen in the same period of prior years. Rohan will provide further details in his remarks.

Encouragingly, we've seen some customers commit to larger multiyear deals following the optimization efforts they undertook last year. This helped accelerate our RPO growth to 31% in the quarter, reflecting the deepening of our customer relationships as they plan for long-term growth. To accelerate use case expansions and support the long-term growth trajectory of our cloud business, we're driving operational enhancements across several areas in the business.

We do appreciate, however, the fact that conservatism and deceleration are already baked into Confluent's outlook (Q3 guidance calls for $281-$282 million in subscription revenue, or 17% growth at the midpoint, reflecting four points of further deceleration versus Q2 actuals)—which to me gives Confluent a chance to surprise to the upside.

Meanwhile, the company continues to land higher-value customers. As shown in the chart below, the company added 9 net-new customers who generate over $1 million in ARR, bringing the total to 219 such customers (+24% y/y).

We also appreciate the fact that despite compression in consumption trends, Confluent continues to boost its margin profile. Pro forma operating margins in Q2 rose 570bps y/y to 6.3%.

In my view, there is also substantial potential for Confluent to outperform its full-year ~6% pro forma operating margin target, considering it already hit these profitability levels in Q2 and can benefit from further efficiency measures.

Key takeaways

In my view, Confluent is a fantastic, overlooked stock. Even if growth decelerates to the high teens, the stock's ~5x forward revenue multiple sits two to three turns below most similar-growth peers, raising the likelihood of a rebound the next time the company gives the markets a beat-and-raise. With an expansive TAM and a growing trend to larger blue-chip clients, this is a secure "Buy."

data.title: Digital Realty Trust Analysis: Financial Health And Opportunity In Preferred Stock
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

In this article, we will take a closer look at Digital Realty Trust, Inc. (NYSE:DLR), its financial condition, and the characteristics of its preferred stock.

Company Overview

Digital Realty Trust, Inc., founded in 2004, provides a full range of data center, colocation, and connectivity solutions, serving leading global companies and service providers. As of the end of the second quarter of 2025, DLR had $48 billion in assets, over 300 data centers, 229000 cross-connects, and more than 5000 customers. The company's capital structure consists of 93% fixed-rate debt and 7% floating-rate debt. The company has an asset coverage ratio of 263% and generates a rental yield of around 11.58%. The asset yield is close to 12%. The current market capitalization is close to $60.5 billion. DLR has a credit rating by the major credit agencies: BBB+ credit rating by S&P, Baa2 by Moody's, and BBB by Fitch.

The common stock of the company is trading at $174.12, currently:

The forward P/FFO is 24.20, so the expected FFO yield comes to 4.13%.

DLR has 20 consecutive years of dividend payments and, for the past 12 months, has paid a $4.88 total dividend. The dividend yield is 2.80% Below, you can see the dividend history of the company:

Here's what the yield looks like over the past five years:

The company has close to 337.5 million shares outstanding, and with a $4.88 forward estimated dividend, it amounts to approximately $1.65 billion in common share distributions for the next four quarters. Here are the forward estimations:

Preferred stocks

All three preferred stocks of DLR are rated with a BBB- credit rating by S&P, BB+ by Fitch, and Baa3 by Moody's. All are traded below par at the time of writing.

(NYSE:DLR.PR.J) is trading at the moment at $22.67 price with a yield to worst of close to 5.79%. It pays a 5.25% cumulative distribution in the form of $0.328125 quarterly distributions, and $1.31 annual amount. DLR issued 8 million shares with a $25 par value each of DLR.PR.J on 08/1/2017 for gross proceeds of $200 million. It is callable anytime - its call date was on 08/07/2022. It has a credit rating of BB+/BB+/Baa3 from S&P, Fitch, and Moody’s.

(NYSE:DLR.PR.L) is currently priced $22.24 and has a yield to worst of 5.85%. It pays a 5.20% cumulative distribution in the form of $0.325 quarterly distributions and $1.30 annual amount. DLR issued 12 million shares with a $25 par value each of DLR.PR.L on 10/01/2019 for gross proceeds of $300 million. It is callable anytime - its call date was on 10/04/2024. It has a credit rating of BB+/BB+/Baa3 from S&P, Fitch, and Moody’s.

(NYSE:DLR.PR.K) is priced at $23.90 at the time of writing and has a yield of worst of 6.10%. The stock pays a 5.85% cumulative distribution per annum in the form of $0.365625 per quarter, and $1.46 annual amount. DLR issued 8 million shares with a $25 par value each of DLR.PR.K on 03/04/2019 for gross proceeds of $250 million. It is callable anytime - its call date was on 03/15/2024. It has a credit rating of BB+/BB+/Baa3 from S&P, Fitch, and Moody’s.

And here is a comparison between the preferred stocks of DLR and its peers in the REIT (real estate investment trust) sector, that trade below par and have high credit ratings:

We can see that DLR.PR.K outperforms all with a higher yield to worst.

DLR shares have many positives - such as high yields, stable dividends, and a credit rating. All shares are under par, which offers the potential for capital gains as the market normalizes and spreads narrow. In addition, DLR is a leader in data centers with >300 sites and >5000 customers, supported by AI and cloud search, which supports long-term stability. But we should also be on the lookout for risks when investing in them - such as sector risks or low liquidity. Preferred shares often have lower trading volume, which can make it difficult to sell quickly without losing money. Always check current market conditions before investing.

OTC bonds

DLR has unsecured bonds, too. We observe in the picture:

Their yields reach 5% and all are rated with a credit rating Baa2 by Moody's.

Conclusion

Digital Realty Trust, Inc. is establishing itself as a leader in the data center sector. The preferred shares are trading below par with high yields, outperforming their peers, and have the potential for capital gains as the market normalizes. However, low liquidity and sector risks require caution.

data.title: Bloomin' Brands May Have A Debt Problem
data.description:
Bloomin' Brands, Inc. (NASDAQ:BLMN) hasn’t had a great year. Its share price was $16.76 a year ago and has now fallen to $6.94 as of September 22, which is a 58 percent drop. Now the question is whether this restaurant holding company can turn itself around. On September 10, it announced that its Outback Steakhouse restaurant would introduce a $12.99 appetizer platter with three items, as well as a new color-changing cocktail that comes with a collectible platypus and platypus egg. It’s also introducing several other new menu items.

It appears that Outback is trying to do what the casual-dining chain Chili’s, which is owned by Brinker International, Inc. (EAT), has done. Chili’s introduced a popular $10.99 meal deal that turned its fortunes around and now several other casual-dining restaurants are introducing similar deals. I wrote about Brinker recently and while Chili’s is still posting great results, Brinker’s stock price has fallen since the article came out. Chili’s still seems like it will continue to perform well in my opinion, but investors are also concerned about the health of the restaurant sector in general. While Bloomin’s stock price has fallen significantly, in this environment it could be a value trap.

Outback is Bloomin Brands’ main restaurant concept, but its other concepts also have enough locations to impact its results. In its report for the second quarter of 2025, it said that it had a total of 1,118 restaurants in the United States. This included 678 Outback Steakhouse restaurants, so Outback made up 61 percent of its domestic holdings. There were also 208 Carrabba's restaurants, 166 Bonefish Grill restaurants, and 65 Fleming’s restaurants, as well as 1 Aussie Grill restaurant. Additionally, there are 343 Outback restaurants outside of the US, including 178 franchised restaurants in Brazil as well as 98 franchised restaurants in South Korea.

Bloomin’ reported mixed results for its restaurants in the second quarter. Outback Steakhouse’s comps were down 0.6 percent. Its Bonefish Grill restaurant reported -5.8 percent same-store sales growth. But comps were up 3.9 percent at Carrabba's and 3.8 percent at Fleming’s. In overall terms, same-store sales were down 0.1 percent, which doesn’t seem that bad. But some of Bloomin’s other financial metrics are more concerning.

Why This Is Not a Good Dividend Stock Right Now

The main appeal of Bloomin’ for the bulls is that it’s trading at a much lower valuation than other restaurants on several metrics. For example, after the market close on September 22 it had a forward P/S ratio of 0.15 versus 0.98 for the restaurant sector. It also had a forward P/E of 7.55 versus 19.81 for the sector, which is a 62 percent discount. But if you go by EV/EBITDA instead, Bloomin’ has an EV/EBITDA of 8.76 versus 10.95 for the median restaurant, which is a 20 percent discount.

The reason why Bloomin’ doesn’t look like as much of a deal based on EV/EBITDA is that it’s carrying a lot of debt, which increases its EV. This company currently has $2.17 billion in debt. This high debt load may be why it hasn’t bought back any stock in 2025, even though its stock price has fallen so much. And other metrics suggest that Bloomin’ could also be having a tough time making interest payments on its debt. Its levered free cash flow for the last twelve months is -$97.4 million.

The company has been trying to reduce its leverage recently. In December 2024, Bloomin’ sold a 67 percent stake in its Brazilian restaurants to the private equity firm Vinci Partners. Bloomin’ is still collecting installment payments from this transaction and is using them to pay off its debt. But its financial reports include the impact of these payments and the royalties from the Brazilian franchise, and even with this income, its levered free cash flow is still negative.

That might be why Seeking Alpha is currently displaying a warning that Bloomin’ is very likely to cut its dividend in the near future. The dividend is costing it too much. Bloomin’ already reduced its quarterly dividend from $0.24 last year to $0.15 earlier this year and has paid dividends of $0.15 per share for the past 3 quarters. But because its stock price has fallen so much recently, at a price of $6.94 per share, its annual dividend of $0.60 still produces an 8.6 percent forward yield, which is very high. Bloomin’ might also need to cut the dividend to pay for its Outback Steakhouse turnaround plan in my opinion, which will include some additional costs.

The Outback Steakhouse Turnaround Plan

Bloomin’ provided more details about its Outback Steakhouse turnaround plan during the second-quarter earnings call. The restaurant has now set up Ziosks at its tables. These kiosks allow guests to pay their checks themselves, which saves time for servers because they don’t have to pick up the checks. Chili’s first installed these devices in 2014 and was using them at 1,100 locations in 2024. So while this is a good step toward efficiency for Outback Steakhouse, it’s something that other restaurants have been doing for quite some time.

The restaurant has also decided to hire one server for every four tables at Outback. Previously, it had reduced the ratio to one server for every six tables under the assumption that giving the servers handheld devices would make them more efficient. But it appears that this hasn’t produced a satisfactory level of service quality. This initiative will increase Outback’s labor costs but it might be worth it if it makes guests happier.

Another turnaround initiative will be remodeling Outback Steakhouse restaurants. It’s unclear how much the remodels will cost because the company is currently testing three remodeling plans. What is clear is that Bloomin’ is focusing on remodeling its Outback restaurants before it adds any new locations. So Bloomin’s revenue isn’t going to rise in the short term because of new restaurants, and any short-term bull case for this company depends on increasing sales at the current Outback locations. Outback is a mature brand, though, so this isn’t a big surprise.

Bloomin’ is also making menu adjustments at Outback. The September 10 press release went into more detail about this initiative. New entrees at Outback include a sirloin and shrimp meal for $24.99, a salad with bacon and blue cheese for $5.99, and a brownie with ice cream for $10.79. There’s also a new chicken entree with bacon and honey mustard sauce for $21.49. And again, the biggest update is the $12.99 appetizer platter with fried onions, French fries, and fried cheese. So the menu updates could attract more value-oriented guests, which is important right now because consumers are trying to conserve their money.

Valuation Estimate for Bloomin’ Brands

If you take the midpoint of Bloomin’s EPS estimate for the full year of 2025, the company now expects adjusted EPS of $1.05. Its full-year 2024 EPS was $0.99 per share. That’s 6 percent EPS growth, which isn’t a lot, and it sounds like revenue and margins are likely to be flat next year. If you assume that no restaurants are added and comps drop by 0.1 percent in 2026, then revenue also drops 0.1 percent. That still rounds up to $1.05 per share.

Bloomin’ currently has 85 million shares outstanding so its projected earnings for 2026 would be $89 million. At a P/E ratio of 7.6, that would produce a market cap of $676 million. Divide that by 85 million shares, because buybacks are unlikely, and that is a share price of $7.95 per share. Again, Bloomin’s closing price on September 22 was $6.94 per share so its share price could rise by 15 percent over the next year if its valuation remains the same. But if the company cuts its dividend, its valuation is likely to fall.

While SA analysts currently rate Bloomin’ as a buy, Wall Street analysts and Seeking Alpha’s quant score currently rate it as a hold. This company may have a low valuation that attracts value investors, but it also has a high debt load and a dividend that appears to be unsustainable, so I don’t think it’s as much of a bargain as it may appear to be.

Conclusion

While Bloomin’ has a plan to turn around Outback Steakhouse that might succeed, it’s too early to invest in the company for that reason. I believe it’s better to wait until other metrics, such as rising same-store sales, indicate that a turnaround is beginning. While it does look like Bloomin’s EPS could rise next year, it also appears that it may have to cut its dividend in my opinion. It did free up some cash by selling a stake in its Brazilian business, but in the long term, it needs to improve its operations to maintain the dividend at this level. So I would say that Bloomin’ is a hold.

data.title: Dow Stock: Crushed 56%, But Positioned For A Powerful Rebound
data.description:
Dow Inc. (NYSE:DOW) stock has been crushed, losing more than 56% in the past year as high energy costs, slowing demand, and broader macroeconomic headwinds weighed on results. As one of the world’s largest chemical producers, Dow is highly cyclical making it vulnerable during downturns but also capable of sharp rebounds when market conditions improve. The key question for investors now: Do today’s depressed market conditions create a compelling long-term buying opportunity in Dow stock?

The Problems For Dow And The Dividend Cut

Dow’s most recent financial results show the challenging landscape for the giant chemical producer. Revenues of $10.23 billion missed estimates by $122 million and were 7.5% lower year-on-year while EBITDA tumbled from $1.4 billion to $745 million. The company has been and is facing a flurry of challenges. Most notably, the company’s end markets such as building materials and automotive have been sluggish and there is an associated pressure from oversupply on the market. This oversupply does not just come from lower demand but is further amplified by the actual supply component where China is oversupplying in key chemical and petrochemical segments. That pressures Dow’s margins.

Those margins had already been under pressure. The chemical industry is an energy-intensive industry and over the past years energy prices have soared. While oil prices have been under pressure which is a positive for naphtha, that only holds when the crack spreads also remain strong which generally is not the case as the macroeconomic outlook and thus demand have dimmed.

The company is one of the largest consumers of ethane as a feedstock for the production of ethylene, which Dow then processes into polyethylene, glycols, elastomers, styrene and PVC.
